paragraphs.text: THIS is, undoubtedly, the golden age of the scientist and explorer. Modern discovery has made transportation and communication between distant points so inexpensive and easy of accomplishment, and new inventions rendered it possible for excavating and prospecting to be carried on so rapidly that work which formerly required years of labor can now be accomplished in a few months. Not many years have passed since the financial world was electrified by news of the discoveries of precious metals at Cripple Creek. That the wonderful fields in that vincinity are not likely to be soon exhausted is proven by the great gold strike just made here. It is believed that the miners have found the dome of a large crater from which immense wealth will be extracted. Sylvanite and rich quartz glitter in florine tale. The vein seems to rise from a chimney in which is a great lake of unknown depth. The decomposed matter is of a bluish color and sparkles with grains of precious metals. Thousands of tons are already in sight.

Wonderful stories reach our ears of the great mineral wealth of the Philippines, and as soon as the government paves a legal way, it will be eagerly trodden by hurrying feet from all parts of the world.

A disvovery of great interest to geologists as well as to seekers for wealth has been announced from Arizona. What is said to be the finest marble in the world has just been found near Camp Bowie by L. R. Legier, of Phoenix, who brought to the knowledge of the world in 1881 the big marble deposit in Gunnison county, Colorado. Mr. Legier has been searching Arizona for marble for 15 year[s.] He has found it in small quantities in almost inaccessible localities, on several occasions, but a few days since he came suddenly upon marble ledges of inestimatable value right where soliders of the regular army have camped for 20 years, totally unconscious of the wealth that lay near them. In one place the largest marble ledge is said to be three miles long on the surface and nowhere has it yet been found to be less than 1,000 feet wide. In this vicinity is marble enough of various kinds to supply the world. A large portion is said to be of finer quality than the best Carrara marble. It is fine and close grained. In a deep gorge about a mile from Camp Bowie the marble is black and surpasses that of Ireland, Belgium or Egypt, formerly the only countries where the stone had been found. This ledge is 300 feet wide. For miles stretches a vein of marbles of all colors and a marble mountain rises here, a great mass.

Soon after gold was discovered in California the gorge was the scene of one of those terrible tragedies which people of the present day of luxurious travel find it hard to comprehend. David Easton started to accompany the family of his fiance, Helen Hight, a beautiful girl, 18 years old, in their long journey across country from Ohio to the coast. While passing through the Chirachuhua mountains, the party traveling with them got far in advance of th[e] Hights, who stopped to repair a broken wheel.

Suddenly a band of Apaches attacked the little company in the rear and all were at once killed or mortally wounded, except Helen and Easton, who were subjected to the most cruel tortures their tormentors could devise. Easton was finally fastened to a cactus bush in such a position that the sharp needles lacerated his flesh to the bone and doomed to thus witness the end of the girl whom he loved. He finally tore himself loose and rushed to the brink of the precipice, over which Indians were holding the girl. As she fell to the rocks, 1,000 feet below, he attacked the murderers and sent four of them to the same doom, then loosened a bowlder which rolled crashing among his pursuers and escaped down a trail, where he was soon afterwards found, wounded and dying, by some of the emigrants who had returned to ascertain the cause of their companions' long delay.
The marble mountain forms a massive monument to the suferers in the tragedy.

There is a mountain of marble near Colton, Cal., where stone for building is quarried. In an excavation at the top large quantities of concrete are manufactured. The Channel islands, off the Pacific coast, nearly opposite this point, present scenes of great interest to the antropologist.

Of these the best known, of course, is Santa Catalina, a resort of such prominence that everyone has more or less knowledge of its strange caves and grottoes, where the remains of an extinct race may be seen.

Heretofore the other islands of this group have been, as a rule, neglected by what is now known as the civilized world. Toward one of these, San Nicolas, public interest is now especially turned, because the University of California is about to begin the most thorough exploration of it ever made. It lies 60 miles off the coast of Santa Barbara and Ventura counties.

Strange presences seem to pervade all of these little islands, where peoples of other days and customs lived, worked and loved. On San Clemente are enormous mortars and pestles and indications that the ancient inhabitants were a giant race.

San Miguel is chiefly interesting from its topography. Great pillars twist around winding caves and rugged canyons suggest attractions to the explorer. Scientists from the Smithsonian institution have secured quantities of ornaments and utensils of bone, shell and stone from Santa Cruz.

On San Nicolas are most abundant evidences of a people of large stature. The islands are seldom visited and loom in lonely desolation on the blue ocean. Science has thus far been able to tell us little of the origin and life of these aboriginal settlers and how and why they disappeared so completely. As far in the past as history deals with the islands skeletons by hundreds have been strewn over San Nicolas. Peculiar stone utensils have been found among the skeletons, indicating a civlization which so far has not been comprehended.

In 1835, 16 members of a strange Indian race were known to live here, and Franciscan padres from the Santa Barbara mission made an attempt to rescue them. A sudden storm drove their sloop from the shore when all the Indians were on board but one woman.
Its occupants were all lost, as the sloop was cast on the rocks of Point Conception.

Traditions existed among the fishermen of a lone woman at the island, and in the [eighteen-] fifties a small expedition landed and found her in a costume of colored bird skins eating blubber from a seal, which she conveyed to her mouth with a bone knife. On seeing the men, she smiled, and falling on her knees, prayed to the sun.

On arriving at Santa Barbara she prostrated herself in adoration before a man on horseback. Two weeks later she died from the effects of food to which she was not accustomed. This was a great disappointment to scientists, who hoped to learn from her lips the traditions of her race. The Island of San Nicolas is ten miles long and four wide at its widest point. It consists of a nearly level plateau at an elevation of 800 to 1,000 feet. Two-thirds of the surface is covered with drifting sand, the remainder affords excellent pasturage for thousands of sheep. Sheep shearers and shell gatherers are chiefest [sic] among its infrequent visitors. Coral Harbor is the only safe landing place and its entrance is but six or seven yards across. White pelicans in large numbers promenade its strip of beach. Circular depressions are the only marks of ancient dwellings of which none of the materials used in construction remain. Immense shell mounds and bones of fish, seals, whales, sea otters, foxes and birds show that the inhabitants were fond of animal food. The quantity of these proves that the population possessed healthy appetites.

Biologists say that the number of varieties of mollusks in San Nicholas are not exceeded in any region of the same area. From one of the elevations, the peaks of all the Channel islands, some 40 miles distant, are visible through the clear atmosphere. On one side of the island is a sparse, petrified stone forest.
